tasks.title: WAVE04 Database: Make bet placement atomic and stop-and-review the remaining accounting surface
tasks.prompt: Finish WAVE04 by turning one high-risk accounting-sensitive workflow into an explicit transaction-scoped unit of work, starting with bet placement unless the touched code reveals an even smaller live compensation path. Define which writes must commit together, where transaction scope begins and ends across repositories, and what primary-DB concurrency protection the touched workflow needs. Preserve integer-first accounting behavior, keep automatic retry or circuit-breaker behavior out of scope, and end the wave with a precise inventory of the remaining sell-position or market-resolution transaction surface while keeping caching or Redis explicitly deferred.
acceptance_criteria: One high-risk accounting-sensitive workflow in the touched code is converted from compensation-style behavior to an explicit transaction-scoped unit of work with clear commit boundaries.
The touched workflow makes its primary-DB concurrency protection explicit and preserves existing accounting behavior without adding generic automatic retry or circuit-breaker behavior.

Focused tests prove atomicity-sensitive behavior for the migrated workflow, and the wave ends with an explicit remaining inventory for other accounting-sensitive flows while keeping caching or Redis deferred.

focus: explicit atomic unit of work for the first accounting-sensitive workflow
deterministic evidence for touched transaction and concurrency behavior
precise remaining accounting-surface inventory with caching still deferred

out_of_scope: a generic transaction framework for every workflow
automatic retry or circuit-breaker rollout around money-moving writes
notes: Start with the smallest high-risk workflow, expected to be bet placement.
Keep Redis and caching explicitly deferred until runtime ownership and accounting correctness are stronger.
tasks.last_summary:
task_uid: `d439e58f-93be-42de-bd8c-1cb05cab0705`  
task_id: `WAVE04-DB-005`  
status: `completed`

Spawned `software-action-dispatcher-agent`; it coordinated specialist review and verifier sign-off.

Changed bet placement to require an explicit transaction unit of work. The GORM adapter now scopes user balance reads/debits and bet insertion to one transaction, with Postgres `FOR UPDATE` protection on the user row. Removed the buy-side compensation ledger path from the active placement workflow and added rollback coverage for failed bet creation.

tasks.title: WAVE05 Security: Make runtime/bootstrap own JWT, proxy-trust, CORS, and header posture
tasks.prompt: Use `lib/design/design-plan.json`, `../socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/05-security-hardening.md`, and `../socialpredict/README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/FUTURE/01-long-term-security-hardening.md` as the binding design context for WAVE05 security hardening. Complete the first useful slice by moving deployment-sensitive security posture behind an explicit runtime/bootstrap seam. Make JWT signing-key presence, trusted-proxy rules for forwarded headers, CORS defaults, and header or HSTS ownership assumptions explicit in runtime code and operator-facing docs; remove ad hoc env reads from auth or server code where the touched path can now depend on injected runtime settings. Preserve current login and serving behavior where safe, fail closed on missing JWT signing material for the touched auth-serving path, and do not create a new `security/*` platform tree.
acceptance_criteria: The touched runtime/bootstrap path owns JWT signing-key presence, trusted-proxy rules, CORS defaults, and header or HSTS posture explicitly instead of leaving them split across ad hoc helpers.
Touched auth or server code no longer reads deployment-sensitive security settings ad hoc when an injected runtime seam is available.
Focused tests and operator-facing note updates cover the chosen runtime security posture and missing-key failure behavior for the touched slice.

focus: runtime ownership of deployment-sensitive security posture
removal of ad hoc env reads from touched auth and server paths
explicit fail-closed JWT key behavior for the touched slice

out_of_scope: distributed rate limiting or compliance-program work
notes: Keep deployment-sensitive security posture separate from application-policy configuration.
Do not hide runtime security ownership back inside `setup`, auth helpers, or a new middleware platform tree.

Runtime now loads and validates JWT signing material, proxy-header trust, CORS defaults, and HSTS/header posture; server/auth consume injected settings; missing JWT signing key fails closed before serving. Operator notes were updated in `README/PRODUCTION-NOTES/BACKEND/05-security-hardening.md`.

tasks.title: WAVE05 Security: Harden client-IP extraction and keep the in-process limiter honest
tasks.prompt: After the runtime-owned proxy rules exist, harden `backend/security` rate limiting around an explicit client-identity extractor that respects the new proxy-trust contract instead of blindly trusting `X-Forwarded-For` or `X-Real-IP`. Keep the current in-process limiter as the active-slice baseline, reuse the boundary failure path for `429` behavior rather than reintroducing transport-rough strings, and make the limitation to per-process enforcement explicit in code, tests, and the active security note without pulling in Redis or shared rate limiting.
acceptance_criteria: The touched rate-limiting path no longer blindly trusts forwarded headers and instead uses an explicit proxy-trust or client-identity contract.
Touched `429` behavior stays on the shared boundary-failure path rather than raw transport strings.
Focused tests and note updates make the in-process limiter baseline and its replica-level limitations explicit for the touched slice.

focus: stable shared `429` behavior for touched middleware paths
explicit in-process limiter baseline without distributed-scope drift

notes: Keep the active-slice limiter local and honest about its limits.
Do not reintroduce plain-text `429` behavior while tightening proxy trust.

tasks.title: WAVE05 Security: Inject shared validation and sanitization into the first auth and write-path slices
tasks.prompt: Keep `backend/security` as shared request-boundary support instead of a hidden constructor call in every handler. Migrate the first high-value touched slices that still instantiate `security.NewSecurityService()` ad hoc, starting with login plus one write-heavy boundary path such as admin add-user or create-market, onto injected or otherwise owned reusable validation and sanitization dependencies through the existing server or container seams. Preserve current validation and sanitization behavior, keep handlers thin, and do not invent a new validation platform or move boundary checks into domain policy.
acceptance_criteria: The touched auth and write-path slices no longer create fresh security services ad hoc when a shared boundary seam is sufficient.
Reusable validation and sanitization ownership is explicit in the touched server, container, or handler wiring without leaking transport checks into domain policy.
Focused tests preserve current validation and sanitization behavior for the migrated slices.

focus: owned validation and sanitization seam reuse
removal of ad hoc `security.NewSecurityService()` creation from touched paths

out_of_scope: new top-level security or validation subsystems
notes: Prefer the smallest reusable seam that removes a real ad hoc security-service construction.
Do not turn `backend/security` into domain policy or a second platform architecture.

tasks.title: WAVE05 Security: Stop and review remaining boundary-security gaps and deferred platform topics
tasks.prompt: Finish WAVE05 with a security-specific stop-and-review point. Remove or document any touched leftover plain-text security failures that still bypass the shared boundary contract, inventory the remaining spoofable-header, raw auth, or ad hoc validation hotspots after this wave, and refresh the active security note so the next queue slice is a precise auth or boundary-hardening seam instead of a vague platform backlog. Keep `FUTURE/01-long-term-security-hardening.md` explicitly deferred and do not pull MFA, RBAC, distributed rate limiting, request signing, session redesign, or compliance work into the active queue.
acceptance_criteria: The touched remaining boundary-security exceptions are either removed or made explicit with a precise next migration seam.
The active security note reflects what this wave actually hardened, what remains, and which long-term topics stay deferred.
The wave ends with a concrete follow-on security or auth seam rather than a broad speculative platform backlog.

focus: continued deferral of long-term security-platform topics

out_of_scope: distributed rate limiting, MFA, RBAC, or request signing implementation
notes: This is the WAVE05 stop-and-review point before broader auth or API convergence work.
Keep security hardening threaded through existing seams instead of reopening a separate platform buildout.
